name: Portal Pair
description: Two linked surfaces or volumes remap position, velocity, orientation, visibility, or objects across space.
design_notes: Portal pairs depend on transform mapping and loop prevention. The design should define portal_pair_id ownership, allowed body classes, teleport exit correction, camera orientation, cooldown after transfer, and how portals interact with projectiles, physics objects, saves, and moving platforms.
design_purpose: Create spatial reasoning puzzles based on non-local movement and line of sight.
Let players redirect momentum, objects, projectiles, or visibility between separated spaces.
Support compact levels that feel larger through transform mapping.
player_fantasy: The player feels they can bend space and solve routes by thinking through linked surfaces.

parameters.name: portal_pair_id
parameters.description: Stable identifier connecting entry and exit portals.

parameters.name: entry_portal_id
parameters.description: Portal endpoint that detects entering bodies and computes source transform.

parameters.name: exit_portal_id
parameters.description: Linked endpoint that receives mapped bodies and camera orientation.

parameters.name: teleport_lockout_ms
parameters.description: Short lockout that prevents immediate re-entry loops after a transfer.

parameters.name: exit_clearance_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule used when the exit volume is obstructed or overlaps a body.

parameters.name: orientation_mapping_mode
parameters.description: Mapping rule for view direction, velocity, and character facing.

edge_cases: An object is partially inside the portal and collides with both rooms during the same step.
Two portals face each other and create an infinite transfer loop.
The exit portal moves, rotates, or becomes blocked while a body is crossing.
A save occurs while a player or object is mid-transfer.
common_bugs: Velocity is mapped but angular velocity, camera pitch, or carried objects are not.
Teleport lockout is global and prevents other actors from using the portal pair.
Collision correction pushes the player back into the entry portal and causes flicker.
Portal rendering shows the correct view but authority collision still uses old room coordinates.
balancing_notes: Portals are easier to reason about when entry and exit surfaces have strong visual identity.
Momentum-preserving portals create expressive solutions but require larger safety margins around exits.
If portals carry enemies or projectiles, combat difficulty can change sharply.
accessibility_notes: Offer comfort options for sudden camera rotation, FOV shifts, and rapid visual recursion.
Use labels, colors plus shapes, or directional arrows so linked endpoints are identifiable.

implementation_notes.unity: Use trigger volumes for endpoints and map transforms through portal-local matrices. Transfer Rigidbody velocity and angular velocity, CharacterController state, camera orientation, and carried object references in one transaction with per-body teleport lockout.
implementation_notes.godot: Represent each endpoint as an Area node with a linked portal_pair_id. Use Transform2D or Transform3D mapping for pose and velocity, and apply exit_clearance_policy_id before committing the new transform.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use portal actors with scene capture or custom rendering for view, but keep gameplay transfer in server or authoritative movement code. CharacterMovement needs correction for orientation, velocity, and penetration at the exit.
implementation_notes.web: In Phaser or Three.js, keep portals as simulation volumes with transform mapping separate from rendering tricks. Store per-object lockout and last portal id to prevent re-entry loops.

relationships.reason: Portal movement and one-way collision rules need clear priority to avoid one-sided trapping or clipping.
Rewind must capture portal entry, exit transforms, and cooldowns to avoid loop divergence.
Lock-on cameras can lose orientation when portals remap character position or facing direction.

example_games.title: Portal
example_games.note: Uses paired portals for spatial reasoning and momentum puzzles.

example_games.title: The Talos Principle
example_games.note: Uses connector-style spatial and line-of-sight logic in puzzle spaces.
